skill.name: web-artifacts-builder
skill.description: Create multi-file React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui projects for Claude artifacts, then bundle them into one shareable HTML file.
skill.summary: Build and package complex React-based Claude artifacts as single HTML files.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity matches, but the weak-crypto detections are false positives from license text, Markdown, and CSS theme names. The confirmed risk is moderate because the skill asks users to run shell scripts that install npm packages, create and modify project files, remove local build outputs, and extract a bundled component archive.

medium_findings.title: Shell Scripts Install Dependencies and Modify Projects
medium_findings.description: The skill workflow tells users to run shell scripts. The initialization script invokes package managers, creates a Vite project, installs dependencies, rewrites configuration files, and extracts a local component archive. The bundling script installs build tools, removes previous build outputs, and writes bundle.html. This matches the skill purpose, but it requires user trust in network package installs and local filesystem changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and file operations are directly present and are central to the skill workflow. They appear legitimate for scaffolding and bundling, but they create real execution, network, and filesystem exposure.

medium_findings.title: Caller-Controlled Project Path Creates Filesystem Scope Risk
medium_findings.description: The initialization script accepts a project name from the caller, passes it to create-vite, and changes into that directory before writing many generated files. The value is quoted, reducing shell injection risk, but users can still choose paths that place generated project files outside the expected working directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path comes from the script argument and controls where project files are created. Shell injection is unlikely because variables are quoted, so the concern is scoped filesystem placement rather than command injection.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography detections occur in Apache license prose, Markdown text, and generated CSS color names such as destructive. No cryptographic primitive, hashing operation, or security-sensitive algorithm use was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows ordinary prose, Markdown, and CSS variable names. There is no evidence of cryptographic code at those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Apache license text, shadcn/ui documentation, and a schema URL written into generated components.json. These are not used to exfiltrate data or make hidden runtime network requests by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are static references in license, documentation, or generated configuration. No evidence found that they receive local files, credentials, or telemetry.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: The scanned skill instructions include workflow and design guidance, but no text was found telling the evaluator to ignore instructions, override security review, claim pre-approval, or assume special authority.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed instruction text is directive for artifact design and testing workflow, not for manipulating the audit process. No evidence found of explicit prompt-injection phrases in the scanned files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts run npm and pnpm commands that can execute dependency lifecycle behavior and fetch packages from registries. This is expected for a frontend scaffold, but it should run only in a trusted workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package manager commands are explicit and repeated. The risk depends on dependency and registry trust, not on hidden malicious logic in the reviewed lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Write and Cleanup Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts write configuration and source files, create bundle.html, and remove dist and bundle.html before building. These operations are normal for this tool, but users should run it from the intended project root.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes and deletion command are directly visible. The deletion is limited to local build paths, so this is a controlled but important operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bundled Archive Extraction
dangerous_patterns.description: The initialization script extracts shadcn-components.tar.gz into the generated src directory. The archive appears intended to provide UI components, but bundled archives should be reviewed before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tar extraction command is explicit and uses a local archive path. The reviewed script does not show malicious intent, but archive contents add supply-chain review scope.

content.value_statement: Complex Claude artifacts often need more structure than a single HTML file. This skill creates a React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui project and bundles it into one shareable artifact.
actual_capabilities: Creates a React and TypeScript project using Vite.
Configures Tailwind CSS 3.4.1 with shadcn/ui theme variables.
Installs Radix UI, lucide-react, form, carousel, and related UI dependencies.
Adds path aliases for @/ imports in TypeScript and Vite configuration.
Extracts bundled shadcn/ui components into the generated source tree.
Bundles a project into one self-contained HTML file with Parcel and html-inline.
limitations: It requires Node.js 18 or newer and a working npm or pnpm environment.
It installs packages from external registries during setup and bundling.
It is intended for complex React artifacts, not simple static HTML snippets.
It does not verify the visual quality or behavior of the generated artifact.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Rich Interactive Artifact
use_cases.description: Build a stateful React artifact with reusable UI components, routing patterns, and Tailwind styling.

use_cases.title: Package a Frontend Demo for Sharing
use_cases.description: Convert a Vite React project into one HTML file that can be shared as a Claude artifact.

use_cases.title: Standardize Component-Based Artifact Work
use_cases.description: Start from a consistent shadcn/ui and Radix UI setup instead of assembling project boilerplate manually.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Artifact Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use web-artifacts-builder to create a new React artifact project named my-dashboard. Keep the first screen focused on the usable interface.

prompt_templates.title: Build With shadcn/ui Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React artifact that uses shadcn/ui buttons, tabs, dialogs, and forms. Use Tailwind CSS for layout and keep the UI responsive.

prompt_templates.title: Bundle an Existing Artifact
prompt_templates.prompt: Bundle the current React artifact project into a single HTML file. Confirm the project has index.html before running the bundling script.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Complex Stateful Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Use web-artifacts-builder to scaffold a React artifact for a multi-step planning tool with local state, component reuse, and polished responsive views. Bundle it when complete.

output_examples.input: Create a project for an interactive dashboard artifact.
output_examples.output: A new React and TypeScript project is created with Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui components, Radix UI dependencies, and configured import aliases.
The project is built and inlined into a single bundle.html file that contains the application assets.
The artifact can use installed UI primitives such as tabs, dialogs, forms, menus, tables, and tooltips.

best_practices: Run the scripts inside a dedicated workspace or throwaway project directory.
Review package changes and generated files before sharing the final artifact.
Use this skill when the artifact needs React state, reusable components, or a build step.
anti_patterns: Do not run the bundling script from a directory that is not the project root.
Do not use this workflow for a small static artifact that can be written as one file.
Do not publish generated artifacts before checking layout, dependencies, and bundled output size.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui project for complex Claude artifacts.

faq.question: Does it produce a single file?
faq.answer: Yes. The bundling script builds the project and writes a self-contained bundle.html file.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does it need internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts install npm and pnpm packages from external package registries.

faq.question: Is this good for simple HTML artifacts?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for larger artifacts that need React, state management, and reusable components.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing?
faq.answer: Users should review installed dependencies, generated files, visual behavior, and the final bundled HTML file.
